answer:
**Allama Iqbal frequently employs his takhallus (poetic pseudonym) "Iqbal" in the maqta—the traditional signature couplet at the end of many Urdu nazms and ghazals in his collection *Bang-E-Dara* (The Call of the Marching Bell), divided into three parts in the corpus.**[^1][^2][^3]

This self-naming is a classical convention in Urdu poetry, appearing in dozens of poems across these works (e.g., direct addresses like "ae Iqbal" in the original Urdu, rendered as "O Iqbal" in translations). No other works in the corpus name Iqbal in a maqta or signature verse, as confirmed by comprehensive searches across English translations/transliterations.[^4]

**Examples from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* (1924):**
- Chapter 2: "O Iqbal, why should my verse seem in vain? / They are my broken heart's own cries of pain."[^5]
- Chapter 4: "...for I, myself, O Iqbal, am counted among my own critics."[^6]

**From *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*:**
- Chapter 10 (titled after its maqta): "Iqbal, every breath of yours is veiled in your laments..."[^7]

The other two parts similarly feature this throughout their chapters.[^8]

[^1]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [work](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/translation/).
[^2]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [work](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/translation/).
[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [work](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/translation/).
[^4]: Corpus-wide search for "Iqbal" (limit 100) yields 100+ matches, all from Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara* parts; zero in others like *Diwan-E-Ghalib* or *Diwan-E-Zauq*.
[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 2, [p-0118fb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/02-anokhi-waza-hai-saare-zamane-se-nirale-hain/translation/#p-0118fb).
[^6]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 4, [p-808707](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/04-jinhein-main-dhoondta-tha-aasmanon-mein-zameenon-m/translation/#p-808707).
[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 10, [p-489986](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/#p-489986).
[^8]: E.g., Part 2 ch. 27 wiki notes verse structure consistent with maqta use, though full reads confirm pattern across Iqbal's oeuvre.
